elements.sent-2411-001: In this letter, we present the first measurement of direct photons at the transverse momentum of $ 1 < \pt < 6$ \GeVc at midrapidity $|\eta| < 0.8$ in inelastic and high-multiplicity proton--proton collisions at a centre-of-mass energy of $\sqrt{s}$ = 13 TeV.
elements.sent-2411-002: The fraction of virtual direct photons in the inclusive virtual photon spectrum is obtained from a fit to the dielectron invariant mass spectrum.
elements.sent-2411-003: In the limit of zero invariant mass, this fraction is equal to the relative contribution of real direct photons in the inclusive real photon spectrum.
elements.sent-2411-004: Contributions from decays of light-flavour neutral mesons are estimated using independent measurements in proton--proton collisions at the same energy and the same event class.
elements.sent-2411-005: The yield of direct photons in inelastic pp collisions is compared to perturbative QCD calculations.
elements.sent-2411-006: The integrated photon yield is studied as a function of charged-particle multiplicity and is compared to the results from other experiments and theoretical calculations.
elements.sent-2411-007: The results show a significant increase of direct-photon yield with charged-particle multiplicity.
elements.sent-2411-008: Extensive studies of ultra-relativistic heavy-ion collisions in the last decades have established the formation of a quark--gluon plasma (QGP) in these collisions, a deconfined state of matter in which quarks and gluons are not bound inside nucleons, see e.g.~Refs.~\cite{ALICE:2022wpn,Braun-Munzinger:2015hba,Busza:2018rrf for recent reviews.}
elements.sent-2411-009: Experimental evidence for the creation of a strongly-interacting QGP has been found in many observations, including collective phenomena described by relativistic hydrodynamics~\cite{Voloshin:2008dg,kolb2004hydrodynamic , energy loss effects of colour-charged probes~\cite{Wiedemann:1200905 , sequential suppression and (re)generation of quarkonium states~\cite{CMS:2012gvv , and electromagnetic radiation consistent with high initial temperatures of several hundred MeV~\cite{PHENIX:2008uif,ALICE:2015xmh,ALICE:2023jef .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-010: The latter signature, thermal photons (i.e.~photons produced by a thermalised medium), plays an important role in the studies of the hot system created in heavy-ion collisions~\cite{Ruuskanen:1991au,Geurts:2022xmk,David:2019wpt .}
elements.sent-2411-011: While the momentum distribution of direct photons (i.e.~photons not originating from hadron decays) is defined by the kinematics of interacting partons, the momentum distribution of thermally produced direct photons is related to the medium temperature, with a black-body-type radiation spectrum following an exponential law $e^{-E/T}$, and blue-shifted by radial flow effects from the rapidly expanding medium~\cite{vanHees:2011vb,dEnterria:2005jvq,Shen:2013vja .}
elements.sent-2411-012: In general, direct photons can be created both in the form of real (massless) photons and in the form of virtual photons with non-zero mass~\cite{Dalitz:1951aj , which convert into a lepton pair (dimuon $\mu^{+}\mu^{-}$ or dielectron e$^{+}$e$^{-}$).}
elements.sent-2411-013: An important class of direct photons are so-called prompt photons produced in initial hard scattering processes between the partons such as quark--antiquark annihilation and quark--gluon Compton scattering.
elements.sent-2411-014: At the transverse momentum of \mbox{\pt $>$ 3 \GeVc this contribution can be well described by perturbative quantum chromodynamics (pQCD) calculations~\cite{Gordon:1993qc .}}
elements.sent-2411-015: Smaller systems such as proton--proton (pp) and proton--nucleus collisions in which a large number of charged particles are produced have recently attracted the interest of the heavy-ion community~\cite{Nagle:2018nvi,Dusling:2015gta,Shuryak:2013ke .}
elements.sent-2411-016: These events exhibit several features which were previously attributed to heavy-ion collisions, including collective effects such as long-range angular correlations and flow~\cite{Grosse-Oetringhaus:2024bwr,Schlichting:2016sqo,CMS:2010ifv,ALICE:2019zfl,ATLAS:2015hzw,CMS:2016fnw or enhanced strangeness production~\cite{ALICE:2016fzo .}}
elements.sent-2411-017: Moreover, also heavy-quark production is found to scale faster than linearly with the charged-particle multiplicity~\cite{ALICE:2015ikl , but no evidence for energy loss effects of high-momentum probes has been observed~\cite{ATLAS:2022iyq,ALICE:2017svf,ALICE:2023plt .}}
elements.sent-2411-018: These observations have sparked active discussions about whether the physics relevant to heavy-ion collisions is also at work in smaller collision systems, what conditions are necessary for nuclear matter to form (a droplet of) QGP, or whether it is possible to explain the observed phenomena through, for example, multiple parton interactions arising from colour reconnection effects~\cite{OrtizVelasquez:2013ofg,Bierlich:2014xba .}
elements.sent-2411-019: Simple considerations suggest that the combination of a small volume created in pp collisions and a high multiplicity of produced charged particles should therefore imply at least a very high energy density system.
elements.sent-2411-020: Studies of small systems should, therefore, provide crucial information needed to establish the transition from ``micro'' to ``macro'' dynamical regimes, which are often treated by quite different theoretical means~\cite{Nagle:2018nvi,Dusling:2015gta .}
elements.sent-2411-021: If a thermalised system is produced in such collisions, a signal of thermal radiation in the form of (virtual) direct photons should be present and contribute to the photon spectrum, especially in the low transverse momentum region of \pt $<$ 3 \GeVc~\cite{Shen:2016zpp .}
elements.sent-2411-022: In this momentum range, pQCD calculations are challenging, so measurements of the direct photon yield in pp collisions have often been used as a reference for thermal radiation from the QGP created in heavy-ion collisions~\cite{PHENIX:2008uif,ALICE:2015xmh,STAR:2016use,PHENIX:2015vek .}
elements.sent-2411-023: However, a possible presence of thermal radiation in pp collisions could complicate the correct interpretation of these results obtained in heavy-ion collisions.
elements.sent-2411-024: At higher transverse momentum of \pt $\gtrsim$ 3 \GeVc, the measurement of direct photons in inelastic pp collisions also provides a valuable test for pQCD calculations.
elements.sent-2411-025: Compared to the RHIC energies, at the LHC energies the contribution from higher-order processes plays a much more important role than that of leading order~\cite{Gordon:1993qc , so the experimental input on direct-photon production is relevant for theoretical models.}
elements.sent-2411-026: So far, the studies of real photons in proton--proton and proton--nucleus collisions at the LHC energies did not reveal significant contributions from direct photons at low transverse momentum~\cite{ALICE:2018mjj,ALICE:2019rtd .}
elements.sent-2411-027: Such studies can be based on the measurements of real photons in electromagnetic calorimeters, or they can be performed by tracking external conversions of real photons to e$^{+}$e$^{-}$ pairs in the detector material.
elements.sent-2411-028: However, these measurements suffer from a large background due to $\pi^{0 \rightarrow \gamma \gamma$ decays with a branching ratio of 98.8\%~\cite{ParticleDataGroup:2022pth .}}
elements.sent-2411-029: This background can be largely suppressed by measuring the contribution from virtual direct photons~\cite{PHENIX:2009gyd,PHENIX:2018che,PHENIX:2012krx , which provide an additional observable compared to real photon measurement --- the invariant mass of the created dielectron pair \mee.}
elements.sent-2411-030: The relation between the real photon yield and the associated dielectron pair production is described by the Kroll-Wada equation~\cite{Kroll:1955zu,Landsberg:1985gaz , which has a $\sim 1/m_{\rm ee}$ dependence of the corresponding dielectron spectrum for $p_{\rm T,ee \gg m_{\rm ee}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-031: The measurement of virtual direct photons allows one to select the mass range $m_{\rm ee \gtrsim m_{\pi^{0 = 135$ \MeVmass, which drastically improves the signal-to-background ratio compared to measurements of real direct photons.}}}
elements.sent-2411-032: The drawbacks of this method are the rapidly decreasing cross section as a function of invariant mass ($\sim 1/m_{\rm ee}$) and the small internal conversion probability of virtual photons ($\sim \alpha_{EM \approx 1/137$).}
elements.sent-2411-033: In this letter we report the first measurement of direct photons in inelastic (INEL) and high-multiplicity (HM) pp collisions at $\sqrt{s =$ 13 TeV at low transverse momentum of $1 < \pt < 6$ \GeVc at midrapitidy $|\eta| < 0.8$ using the virtual photon method.}
elements.sent-2411-034: Compared to the previous publication~\cite{ALICE:2018gev , the present analysis utilises an approximately four times larger statistics of pp collisions and the recent measurements of $\pi^{0}$ and $\eta$ yields in the same event classes~\cite{ALICE:2024XXXX .}}
elements.sent-2411-035: The paper is organised as follows: the ALICE apparatus and the data samples are described in Section~\ref{sec:alice , the data analysis and the cocktail of known hadronic sources are discussed in Section~\ref{sec:data , and the results are presented in Section~\ref{sec:results .}}}
elements.section-2411-002: The ALICE detector and data samples
elements.sent-2411-036: A detailed description of the ALICE apparatus and its performance can be found in Refs.~\cite{ALICE:2004fvi,ALICE:2005vhb,ALICE:2008ngc,ALICE:2014sbx .}
elements.sent-2411-037: Below, we briefly describe the detectors used in this analysis to reconstruct and identify dielectrons.
elements.sent-2411-038: The reconstruction of charged-particle tracks in the ALICE central barrel relies on the information from the Inner Tracking System (ITS)~\cite{ALICE:2010tia and the Time Projection Chamber (TPC)~\cite{Alme:2010ke .}}
elements.sent-2411-039: Both of these detectors are located inside a solenoid, providing a uniform magnetic field of 0.5 T parallel to the beam axis.
elements.sent-2411-040: The ITS comprises 6 cylindrical layers of silicon detectors located at radial distances from the beam axis between 3.9 cm and 43 cm.
elements.sent-2411-041: The ITS is surrounded by the TPC, the main tracking device in the ALICE central barrel.
elements.sent-2411-042: It is a 5 m long cylindrical gaseous detector extending from 85 cm to 247 cm in the radial direction.
elements.sent-2411-043: It provides up to 159 spacial points per track for charged-particle reconstruction and particle identification (PID).
elements.sent-2411-044: The latter relies on the measurement of the specific energy loss d$E$/d$x$ in the gas volume, which was filled with an Ar/CO$_{2}$ gas mixture (with abundances of 88/12) in 2016 and 2018 and with Ne/CO$_{2}$/N$_{2}$ (90/10/5) in 2017.
elements.sent-2411-045: The PID is complemented by the Time-of-Flight (TOF)~\cite{ALICE:2000xcm system placed at a radial distance of 3.7 m from the beam axis.}
elements.sent-2411-046: It provides the arrival time of particles relative to the event collision time.
elements.sent-2411-047: The latter can be measured either by the TOF detector itself or by the T0 detector comprising two arrays of Cherenkov counters at forward rapidities~\cite{ALICE:2016ovj .}
elements.sent-2411-048: Collision events are triggered by the V0 detector, a set of two plastic scintillator arrays placed on both sides of the interaction point at forward rapidities.
elements.sent-2411-049: Together with the two innermost layers of the ITS detector, the V0 is also used to reject background events such as beam--gas interactions, collisions with de-bunched protons or with mechanical structures of the beam line.
elements.sent-2411-050: The analysis employs the full statistics of pp collisions at $\sqrt{s = 13$ TeV recorded during the Run 2 of the LHC operation between 2015 and 2018.}
elements.sent-2411-051: Simultaneous signals in both V0 arrays synchronised with the beam crossing time are used as a minimum-bias (MB) trigger, that samples $72\%$ of the total inelastic cross section~\cite{ALICE-PUBLIC-2021-005, TOTEM:2017asr .}
elements.sent-2411-052: High charged-particle multiplicity events are triggered by the additional requirement that the total V0 signal amplitude exceeds a certain threshold.
elements.sent-2411-053: At the analysis level, $0.1\%$ of MB events (i.e.~$0.072\%$ of all inelastic events) with the highest V0 multiplicity are selected to define the high-multiplicity class.
elements.sent-2411-054: The primary interaction vertex is reconstructed with the track segments in the two innermost layers of the ITS detector, and pileup events (with multiple reconstructed vertices) are removed from the analysis.
elements.sent-2411-055: The vertex position along the beam axis is required to be within $\pm 10$ cm of the nominal interaction point to ensure a uniform detector coverage in the $|\eta| < 0.8$ range.
elements.sent-2411-056: In total 1.73 \tenpow{9 minimum-bias and \mbox{3.38 \tenpow{8 high-multiplicity \pp events are selected for the analysis, corresponding to integrated luminosities of \mbox{\ensuremath{\mathcal{L ^{\rm {MB _{\rm{int }\xspace = $29.8 \pm 0.6$ \nbarn and \ensuremath{\mathcal{L ^{\rm {HM _{\rm{int }\xspace = $5.8 \pm 0.1$ \pbarn, respectively.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-057: The luminosity determination is based on the visible cross section observed by the V0 trigger measured in a van der Meer scan~\cite{ALICE-PUBLIC-2021-005 .}

elements.sent-2411-060: The electron and positron identification is based on the complementary information from the TPC and the TOF detectors, where the latter suppresses kaon and proton contamination in the momentum ranges in which the TPC \dEdx signals of these hadrons overlap with those of electrons.
elements.sent-2411-061: All electron and positron candidates are paired to form combinations with opposite ($N_{+-}$) and same-sign charges ($N_{\pm \pm}$).

elements.sent-2411-147: In summary, in this letter we have presented direct-photon measurements in inelastic and high-multiplicity pp collisions at $\sqrt{s = 13$ TeV.}
elements.sent-2411-148: With increased statistics and significantly reduced systematic uncertainties of the hadronic cocktail compared to the previous publication~\cite{ALICE:2018gev , we measure for the first time a significant direct photon yield at low transverse momentum in pp collisions at the LHC energies.}
elements.sent-2411-149: The direct photon signal is seen in both event classes that were studied.
elements.sent-2411-150: The results in the inelastic event class are found to be consistent with pQCD-based calculations, while the lack of theoretical predictions prevents a conclusion on the possible presence (and yield) of thermal radiation in high-multiplicity pp collisions.
elements.sent-2411-151: However, from simple scaling of pQCD-based calculations for inelastic pp collisions, it was found that the best description of high-multiplicity data is obtained with the sum of thermal photons (calculated for the HM event class as described in Ref.~\cite{Shen:2016zpp ) and the prompt-photon contribution scaled by a factor of 4.5–7.5 from inelastic pp collisions.}
elements.sent-2411-152: The large uncertainty in the scaling factor reflects the still sizeable uncertainties in the currently available experimental high-multiplicity data.
elements.sent-2411-153: The multiplicity range covered in the present analysis is of particular interest in the context of the search for a possible onset of thermal radiation in small systems.
elements.sent-2411-154: The statistics that will be collected during the Run 3 and Run 4 periods of LHC operation will allow one to study the direct photon yield with higher statistical precision and in a more differential way.
elements.sent-2411-155: The results presented in this Letter and the future results from Run 3 data should stimulate the interest of the theoretical community to calculate the yield of direct photons in small systems as a function of charged-particle multiplicity to shed light on the possible presence of a QGP in small systems.
